attachment.content:
- agentic-swe-digest: Research and deliver a recurring technical digest on agentic software engineering, agent orchestration, agentic SWE, and SWE-bench. Use when Codex needs to gather recent high-signal developments from the last N days, including luminary X/Twitter discourse and new interviews worth watching, save a structured Markdown report under the user-level Codex reports directory, and optionally email the digest to the user's configured Gmail inbox.
- arxiv-to-md: Convert arXiv papers to Markdown using a lightweight local Python script backed by the arxiv2md.org API. Use when a user provides an arXiv ID or arXiv URL, asks to save an arXiv paper as Markdown in the current project, or a DeepDive research packet needs source-text Markdown for arXiv paper captures.
- chrome-session-scraper: Launch and use the Chrome CDP PowerShell tooling in the Chrome Session Scraper repo. Use when Codex is working in this repo and needs to: start a dedicated debug-enabled Chrome profile, scrape content from tabs already open in the authenticated browser session, export one open ChatGPT/OpenAI conversation to markdown, bulk-export specific ChatGPT sidebar threads into markdown files, or capture a standalone local HTML packet into task-owned Markdown plus preserved source HTML.
- codex-repo-operator: Operate the user-level C:\Users\gregs\.codex repo safely. Use before Codex reads, edits, stages, commits, pushes, cleans, ignores, reorganizes, or audits anything under C:\Users\gregs\.codex, especially sessions, skills, orchestration docs, reports, setup files, git state, or cleanup tasks.
- codex-session-search: Search local Codex session transcripts and thread metadata to recover prior commands, paths, decisions, and outcomes. Use when the user refers to an older Codex thread, asks whether work was done before, wants instructions recovered from past sessions, or needs help locating the thread that mentioned a toolchain, error, path, or build command.
- commit: Create a well-formed git commit from current changes using session history for rationale and summary; use when asked to commit, prepare a commit message, or finalize staged work.
- gemini-web-operator: Operate a live Google Gemini conversation through a Chrome debug session when Codex needs to inspect Gemini tabs, attach local images, submit prompts, run image-generation iterations, download full-sized Gemini outputs, or preserve Gemini prompt/result artifacts without relying on screenshots.
- gmail-digest-email: Send a research digest to a preconfigured Gmail inbox through a local MCP server. Use when Codex has completed research on a topic and needs to email the digest to the user's fixed Gmail address, especially for recurring or daily digest workflows. Prefer Markdown-formatted digests so the email can render as styled HTML with plain-text fallback.
- gmail-operator: Shared, multi-account Gmail access via IMAP/SMTP app passwords (no OAuth, no browser). Search/read mail, save attachments, stage drafts (with attachments) in Drafts, and SMTP-send self-notifications - for ARBITRARY accounts selected by name. Credentials live in a gitignored accounts store, never in the repo. Use from any agent/repo to find past emails, draft mail for a human to review and send, or send a self-notification.
- jobs-operator: Create, change, inspect, and trigger recurring or scheduled local jobs through the Codex Orchestration backend (Go control plane + Temporal) on this machine. Use whenever the human asks to set up, repair, disable, or verify a scheduled task / cron job / recurring automation locally — e.g. a daily digest or a monthly billing reconciliation. The canonical path is a declarative v1 job spec reconciled into Temporal; do NOT hand-roll Windows Scheduled Tasks for user jobs.
- justify-local-git-changes: Justify local git diffs with concise behavioral reasons, evidence, and a durable CHANGE-JUSTIFICATION-*.md document. Use when Codex is asked to explain, defend, audit, or prepare justification for unstaged, staged, or remaining local changes, especially shared engine/vendor files or changes that need task/spec traceability.
- notion-operator: Export a Notion page or database to Markdown from an authenticated Chrome debug session. Use when Codex needs to mirror Notion docs (a single page, or a whole database/page tree recursively) into local Markdown — faithfully, including collapsed-toggle and subpage content that DOM scraping misses. Drives Notion's own export API over CDP, downloads the zip through the logged-in browser session, and unzips Markdown locally.
- physical-agents-digest: Research and deliver a recurring technical digest on sensors, actuators, physical and embodied AI, home automation, edge perception, robotics, and real-world control loops that Codex could plausibly supervise or extend. Use when Codex needs to gather recent high-signal developments, save a structured Markdown report under the user-level Codex reports directory, and optionally email it to the user's configured Gmail inbox.
- process-invocation: Resolve and invoke shared orchestration process references before acting. Use when a user instruction, task artifact, plan, prompt, repo doc, or durable Markdown document mentions an "X process", "X workflow", "X coordinator", or "X worker" where X is CamelCase/PascalCase or otherwise process-like, including DeepDive, TaskCreate, TaskResearch, PortToUnreal, CodeDuplication, SolutionCreate, or similar, and Codex must find the explicit orchestration docs rather than infer behavior.
- rustfire-pie-cdb-debugging: Launch or attach cdb.exe for Rustfire Play-In-Editor debugging, especially CombatMap repros that must run while the Unreal Editor stays in the background. Use when Codex needs a repeatable debugger workflow, the background-safe GameAutomation PIE lane, or log checks that distinguish background-throttle failures from real NetworkPrediction or Mover bugs.
- ue-determinism-digest: Research and deliver a recurring technical digest on Unreal Engine determinism, rollback networking, fixed-frame simulation, Chaos, Network Prediction, Iris, Mover, Mass/StateTree, replay/debugging, and adjacent game-engine innovations. Use when Codex needs to gather recent high-signal developments, save a structured Markdown report under the user-level Codex reports directory, and optionally email it to the user's configured Gmail inbox.
- unreal-linux-server-build: Start, monitor, and summarize canonical Rustfire packaged Windows client and Linux dedicated server BuildGraph builds on Windows. Use when Codex needs a packaged Win64 client or Linux server build; Rustfire canonical package builds must use Engine/Build/Graph/Tasks/BuildAndTestProject.xml nodes instead of direct BuildCookRun recipes.
- windows-toast-notification: Send a Windows tray-style toast notification from the local machine and install a watcher that fires when a specific process exits. Use when Codex needs to alert the user that a build, script, background job, or long-running command finished, or when the user explicitly asks for a Windows toast, tray notification, desktop alert, or "notify me when it's done" workflow.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Work complete. Here's the summary.
